Research best practices for UX/Product specification documents in 2025-2026. Focus on:

1. **User Persona Best Practices**:
   - JTBD (Jobs to Be Done) methodology
   - Persona documentation standards
   - Validation techniques

2. **User Journey Mapping**:
   - Modern journey mapping techniques
   - Service blueprinting
   - Customer experience mapping standards

3. **Information Architecture**:
   - IA documentation best practices
   - Navigation patterns for enterprise applications
   - Progressive disclosure principles

4. **Design System Documentation**:
   - Design token systems
   - Component documentation standards
   - Dark mode specification best practices
   - Blueprint.js usage patterns

5. **Screen Specification Standards**:
   - Wireframe documentation
   - Acceptance criteria formats
   - Responsive design specifications

Search the web for current (2025-2026) best practices from sources like Nielsen Norman Group, Figma design community, and enterprise UX leaders. Return what a Senior UX Designer/Product Manager would expect in a specification document.

Based on the search results, here's an overview of information architecture and enterprise applications in 2025:

## Enterprise Architecture Trends for 2025

### Key Frameworks and Approaches

TOGAF is one of the most well-established EA frameworks, providing a step-by-step repeatable process for designing, implementing, and managing enterprise architecture.(no content)Known for its ADM (Architecture Development Method), TOGAF helps organizations develop EA in an iterative, structured way through phases including Architecture Vision, Business Architecture, and Information Systems Architectures which develops data architecture and application architecture to ensure proper system integration.(no content)The Zachman Framework helps organizations classify, structure, and analyze their architecture, and is best for companies that need a clear, structured way to document EA components rather than a step-by-step implementation process.

### Information Architecture in the AI Age

By strategically managing information, enterprises create the foundation for artificial intelligence (AI), analytics, and digital transformation. At its core, Information Architecture is about designing the structure of data so it can be accessed, understood, and applied.(no content)Information Technology Enterprise Architecture (ITEA) aligns IT infrastructure with business strategy, ensuring that applications, databases, and IT processes all reinforce the information strategy, enabling cost savings, scalability, and agility.

### Major Trends for 2025

1. **AI Integration**: Generative AI, large language models (LLMs), and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) are set to revolutionize how EAs operate in 2025. These technologies could enable organizations to extract insights faster, roadmap future scenarios, and design more impactful strategies.

2. **Digital Twins**: In 2025, Digital Twins of Organizations (DTOs) are expected to gain significant traction, enabling businesses to create digital replicas of their operations by integrating real-time data including application performance, financial metrics, customer interactions, employee activities, and supply chain movements.

3. **Agentic AI**: Throughout 2025, enterprise software providers have launched and spoken of agentic AI, claiming it will revolutionise the operating model of organisations. With agents available in key enterprise SaaS applications, organisations require visibility and an ability to plan for, protect and manage agents effectively.

4. **Business-Driven Approaches**: EA has shifted its focus from IT-centric frameworks to business outcome-driven (BODEA) approaches. Organizations have moved away from static documentation and toward dynamic, data-driven modeling to capture real-time insights, with EA increasingly aligned with value stream mapping.

5. **Data Management Evolution**: The landscape of enterprise data management is being reshaped by shifts in strategy, architecture, platform focus, and the convergence of data and knowledge management teams. Organizations are moving from siloed approaches to a more connected, enablement-driven model by leveraging AI-powered tools, self-service capabilities, and evolving governance practices.

### Enterprise Application Architecture

Enterprise Application Architecture (EAA) is a structured approach that ensures all software applications within a company work together efficiently. EAA integrates applications, reduces redundancy, and automates workflows, saving time and costs.(no content)Modern businesses use diverse applications that can create inefficiencies without unified architecture. EAA integrates systems, enabling seamless workflows and boosting digital transformation success by 40%.

Based on my search results, here's what I found about Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) and persona documentation:

## Overview of JTBD and Personas

Jobs-to-be-done (JTBD) is a framework based on the idea that whenever users "hire" (i.e., use) a product, they do it for a specific "job" (i.e., to achieve a particular outcome).(no content)Jobs-to-be-done are typically summarized in a single sentence describing what the user needs to accomplish, and any important context that might impact this job.

## Key Components of JTBD Documentation

Effective JTBD research examines three crucial dimensions: Functional Jobs represent the practical tasks customers need to accomplish (e.g., "organize team deliverables"). Emotional Jobs capture the feelings customers want to experience or avoid (e.g., "feel confident about project delivery"). Social Jobs reflect how customers want to be perceived by others (e.g., "be seen as an organized leader").(no content)A JTBD description typically captures the functional success criteria (the objective, clear requirements for this job to be successful), as well as the emotional success criteria (which may be further broken down into the users' individual emotional criteria, and any social considerations).

## JTBD vs. Personas: The Debate

Product teams at some of the world's most successful enterprises—including Microsoft, GitLab, and Home Depot—have incorporated Jobs to Be Done into their development process, citing the framework as a source of innovation and customer value.(no content)Personas answer the 'who,' while JTBD addresses the 'what' of what customers want, along with the situation, context, and solution.(no content)The JTBD framework, in theory, frames the problem to be solved in a broad way, agnostic of specific stereotypes, allowing wide-reaching solutions to be tested and put into market.

## Integration: JTBD-Infused Personas

Many experts now recommend combining both approaches:

The most successful organizations don't treat JTBD and personas as competing methodologies. Instead, they synthesize their strengths into what we call a unified approach that captures both the demographic precision of traditional personas and the motivational clarity of Jobs to be Done research.(no content)Some UX researchers, like Nikki Anderson-Stanier, believe that JTBD can be used in tandem with personas: "When you put these two concepts together, it is usually an either/or conversation. They don't seem to be able to exist together, in harmony. Most people believe you either do JTBD research or you have personas. I, however, disagree. I believe there is space for a JTBD persona."(no content)Alan Cooper notes: "Take out personas, and JTBD will lead you into a hard-headed and cold analysis. But, on the other hand, without jobs, you lose sight of your product's primary purpose. So probably the best way is to use both methods — Jobs to be Done and personas."

## Practical Application

Combining personas with the Jobs to Be Done (JTBD) framework helps you understand not just who your customers are, but also why they need your products. This way, you cover all bases, from their emotions to practical needs.(no content)JTBD is only as good as the research that's behind it. Both frameworks require solid user research to be effective.

Here are the search results for "design system documentation design tokens 2025":

## Key Findings

### Design Tokens Specification Reaches Stable Version (October 2025)

The Design Tokens Community Group announced the first stable version of the Design Tokens Specification (2025.10) on October 28, 2025, marking a milestone for design systems teams and tool makers worldwide.(no content)"The specification unlocks interoperability across design tools and code," said Kaelig Deloumeau-Prigent, co-chair of the Design Tokens Community Group. The stable specification introduces critical capabilities including theming and multi-brand support, modern color specification with full support for Display P3, Oklch, and CSS Color Module 4 spaces, rich token relationships, and cross-platform consistency.(no content)The specification's stability is backed by reference implementations in leading tools including Style Dictionary, Tokens Studio, and Terrazzo. More than 10 design tools and open-source projects – including Penpot, Figma, Sketch, Framer, Knapsack, Supernova, and zeroheight – already support or are implementing the standard.

### Design Token Format Specification

The specification uses JSON as its interchange format based on ubiquity, human-readability, and widespread tooling support. Design token files use the recommended media type application/design-tokens+json and file extensions .tokens or .tokens.json. All specification-defined properties are prefixed with the dollar sign ($), including required properties like $value and $type, plus optional properties for $description, $extensions, and $deprecated.

### Documentation Tools and Resources

Documentation tools can display design token names rather than the raw values in design specs and style guides. It is often desirable for design system teams to integrate such tools together, so that design token data can flow between design and development tools.(no content)Full documentation, examples, and implementation guidance are available at designtokens.org.

### Token Management Tools for 2025

Zeroheight is the design system documentation platform that evolved into a token powerhouse. It manages tokens alongside component documentation, living guidelines, and handoff workflows all in one place. Perfect for teams who want tokens as part of a larger, documented design system.(no content)Knapsack is the enterprise-grade choice when you need not just token management but full design system governance. It manages design tokens, components, documentation, and permissions in one platform—with an emphasis on real-time collaboration and preventing design-code drift at the source.

### Government Design Systems

The VA.gov Design System uses design tokens to express design decisions by applying the many color, typography, and spacing options available as design tokens from the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS) to specific contexts within their platforms. Design tokens allow them to propagate those design decisions to their platforms.(no content)There are three types of design tokens in the VA Design System: Primitives (building blocks from USWDS), Semantic tokens (which communicate how a primitive is used and capture semantic meaning), and Component tokens (scoped to specific components representing decisions specific to that component).

Here are the best practices for user journey mapping and service blueprinting in 2025:

## Key Differences Between the Two Tools

Journey maps visualize the customer's perspective across touchpoints, while service blueprints go deeper, mapping the internal processes, systems, and roles that support those interactions.(no content)Think of service blueprints as a sequel to customer-journey maps.(no content)The goal of customer-journey maps is to better understand the end users' journey, including their thoughts and emotions. In contrast, service blueprints reflect the organization's perspective and include frontstage actions, backstage actions, and support processes.

## 2025 Best Practices for User Journey Mapping

Journey mapping has evolved in 2025—AI, emotion and real-time orchestration now power customer experience strategy. Customer journey mapping has evolved from one-off diagrams to real-time orchestration tools that drive measurable ROI and customer loyalty.(no content)What makes a journey map effective now is behavioral specificity. "Instead of broad stages like 'awareness' and 'consideration,' we're mapping actual customer behaviors: Did they browse but not add to cart? Did they subscribe to SMS but never open an email? Did they buy once and ghost us? Each of these micro-behaviors triggers a different journey."(no content)Modern journey maps combine behavioral data, emotional cues, and AI-powered insights to uncover what truly drives customer satisfaction.

### Core Elements to Include

When creating a customer journey map, include: Personas (general groups of customers based on demographics and psychographics), Actions (what the user does at each touchpoint), and Timeline (the process of going through the touchpoints and phases of the journey).

### Getting Started Best Practices

To get started with user journey mapping, follow these best practices:
- Define the scope and goals clearly
- Gather data and insights through research, interviews, and surveys
- Involve stakeholders from across the organization
- Keep it simple—start with a simple user journey map and gradually add complexity
- Continuously iterate and refine based on new data and insights

## Best Practices for Service Blueprinting

Like customer-journey maps, blueprints render complex scenarios spanning many service-related offerings. Blueprinting is an ideal approach to experiences that are omnichannel, involve multiple touchpoints, or require a crossfunctional effort.(no content)The most common goal of a service blueprint is to give an organization a comprehensive understanding of its service and the underlying resources and processes. This can be used to discover weaknesses—a blueprint acts as a treasure map that helps businesses visualize exactly who is doing what, when. In many cases, the greatest insight offered is what the business is not doing and where it does not support its customers.

## Best Practices for Synchronizing Both Maps

Best practices for synchronizing both maps include:
1. **Cross-functional Workshops**: Conducting workshops that bring together stakeholders from both service design and UX teams
2. **Shared Goals**: Establishing common objectives for both maps ensures aligned efforts
3. **Iterative Alignment**: Regularly revisiting and updating both maps to keep them in sync as services evolve(no content)Use both together to align customer insight with operational execution.

## When to Use Which Tool

If the nature of your challenges is more about your company's internal processes, then a service blueprint is likely your best bet. If you're looking to understand the customer's experience, then you're headed more toward a customer journey map.(no content)If you're looking for quick wins, a customer journey map could be good to start with as it is relatively less complex.(no content)The frequency of updates depends on changes in the business environment, customer behavior, or service process. However, regular reviews are recommended for continued relevancy.

Here are the best practices for user journey mapping and service blueprinting in 2025:

## Key Differences Between the Two Tools

Journey maps visualize the customer's perspective across touchpoints, while service blueprints go deeper, mapping the internal processes, systems, and roles that support those interactions.(no content)Think of service blueprints as a sequel to customer-journey maps.(no content)The goal of customer-journey maps is to better understand the end users' journey, including their thoughts and emotions. In contrast, service blueprints reflect the organization's perspective and include frontstage actions, backstage actions, and support processes.

## 2025 Best Practices for User Journey Mapping

Journey mapping has evolved in 2025—AI, emotion and real-time orchestration now power customer experience strategy. Customer journey mapping has evolved from one-off diagrams to real-time orchestration tools that drive measurable ROI and customer loyalty.(no content)What makes a journey map effective now is behavioral specificity. "Instead of broad stages like 'awareness' and 'consideration,' we're mapping actual customer behaviors: Did they browse but not add to cart? Did they subscribe to SMS but never open an email? Did they buy once and ghost us? Each of these micro-behaviors triggers a different journey."(no content)Modern journey maps combine behavioral data, emotional cues, and AI-powered insights to uncover what truly drives customer satisfaction.

### Core Elements to Include

When creating a customer journey map, include: Personas (general groups of customers based on demographics and psychographics), Actions (what the user does at each touchpoint), and Timeline (the process of going through the touchpoints and phases of the journey).

### Getting Started Best Practices

To get started with user journey mapping, follow these best practices:
- Define the scope and goals clearly
- Gather data and insights through research, interviews, and surveys
- Involve stakeholders from across the organization
- Keep it simple—start with a simple user journey map and gradually add complexity
- Continuously iterate and refine based on new data and insights

## Best Practices for Service Blueprinting

Like customer-journey maps, blueprints render complex scenarios spanning many service-related offerings. Blueprinting is an ideal approach to experiences that are omnichannel, involve multiple touchpoints, or require a crossfunctional effort.(no content)The most common goal of a service blueprint is to give an organization a comprehensive understanding of its service and the underlying resources and processes. This can be used to discover weaknesses—a blueprint acts as a treasure map that helps businesses visualize exactly who is doing what, when. In many cases, the greatest insight offered is what the business is not doing and where it does not support its customers.

## Best Practices for Synchronizing Both Maps

Best practices for synchronizing both maps include:
1. **Cross-functional Workshops**: Conducting workshops that bring together stakeholders from both service design and UX teams
2. **Shared Goals**: Establishing common objectives for both maps ensures aligned efforts
3. **Iterative Alignment**: Regularly revisiting and updating both maps to keep them in sync as services evolve(no content)Use both together to align customer insight with operational execution.

## When to Use Which Tool

If the nature of your challenges is more about your company's internal processes, then a service blueprint is likely your best bet. If you're looking to understand the customer's experience, then you're headed more toward a customer journey map.(no content)If you're looking for quick wins, a customer journey map could be good to start with as it is relatively less complex.(no content)The frequency of updates depends on changes in the business environment, customer behavior, or service process. However, regular reviews are recommended for continued relevancy.

## UX Specification Document Best Practices (2025-2026)

### What is a UX Design Specification Document?

Design specifications are detailed documents that outline essential requirements, constraints and specifications for products to adhere to. They act as fundamental blueprints in product development, guiding teams from concept to completion. Specifications ensure that all stakeholders—including designers, developers and clients—have a clear understanding of the product goals, to align efforts and facilitate a smoother development process.

### Key Components of a UX Spec Document

A design specifications document usually includes the project overview, scope, goals, roadmap, and possible delivery time or deadline. It also covers typography or character styles, font and background colors, measurements, and other details that define the exact properties of the UI design.(no content)UX design specifications communicate UI design details such as color schemes, styles and measurements, as well as user flows and functionality to developers. They use this documentation to build the digital product based on the designer's specs.

### Best Practices for Creating UX Documentation

**1. Organize from General to Specific**
Start with the big picture like project goals, design principles, user needs before zooming into the finer details like components or error states.

**2. Document Design Rationale**
While a spec document outlines the "what" of UX design, your design rationale explains why you did what you did. Documenting your design rationale involves logging all key decisions relating to the product or feature you're working on. Your design rationale is likely in a constant state of flux as you build, so meticulously logging your design decisions is essential.

**3. Include Wireframes and Mockups**
Wireframes are the backbone of your product. They show the basic structure and hierarchy of key UI elements and, depending on the level of fidelity, can be gray-scale or convey some of the design aesthetics. With the first low-fi wireframe you can test the design with users and get the feedback you need to iterate. As part of UX documentation, wireframes can also serve as a reference point for functional specifications.

**4. Document Component States**
Next to the example views, neatly document every component that is specific to that workflow or category. Every possible state and variant of these components should be found here with a short description explaining when, why, and how they are used. Documenting all component states directly next to their example views, rather than on a separate page, makes them both easy to find and easy to understand.

**5. Make Documentation Accessible**
Get meta and document the documentation. As a UX designer, you're used to the tools you use every day, but not everyone knows all the features inside out. To make your documentation inclusive and transparent, tell people how to use the software and where to find what.

### 2025-2026 Trends Impacting UX Specifications

**AI Integration**
AI is changing how UX work is paced and structured. At UXLx 2025, John Whalen demonstrated how a research process that traditionally took 35 days over seven weeks could be completed in under a week with AI support. From planning to synthesis and reporting, AI transformed every stage of the customer research process—not by replacing human judgement, but by accelerating it.

**Sustainability Guidelines**
The upcoming Web Sustainability Guidelines (WSG) from the W3C define best practices for designing and building planet-friendly digital products. Expected to be finalized in April 2026, the WSG extend sustainability beyond infrastructure and performance, covering UX, development, and strategic decision-making. Their goal is to promote experiences that are clean, efficient, accessible, resilient, and aligned with long-term environmental responsibility.

**Multimodal Interaction**
Good UX design in 2026 considers context first. People use products while walking, cooking, driving, or switching devices, often in environments where typing is slow, awkward, or impossible. Users may start a task by tapping, continue it by speaking, and finish it on another device altogether. Interfaces that only work with touch or require full visual attention might become obsolete.

### Essential Tools for UX Designers (2025-2026)

UI/UX designers have access to a wide range of tools and resources to streamline their design workflows and enhance collaboration. Some essential tools include prototyping and wireframing tools like Adobe XD, Sketch, Figma, and InVision, as well as design systems and libraries for consistent design implementation.

### Why UX Documentation Matters

UX documentation is a vital asset for enhancing collaboration within cross-functional teams. It can be a critical resource during handoffs, whether from designers to developers, or between teams when ownership shifts. It ensures that the reasoning behind design decisions is preserved, reducing back-and-forth and helping new contributors get up to speed faster.

Here are the search results for Blueprint.js design system documentation patterns:

## Blueprint.js Overview

Blueprint is a React-based UI toolkit for the web. It is optimized for building complex, data-dense web interfaces for desktop applications which run in modern browsers. This is not a mobile-first UI toolkit.(no content)Blueprint is an open source project developed at Palantir.

## Documentation Patterns

Much of Blueprint's documentation lives inside source code as JSDoc comments in .tsx files and KSS markup in .scss files. This documentation is extracted and converted into static JSON data using documentalist.(no content)If you are updating documentation sources (not the docs UI code which lives in packages/docs-app or the docs theme in packages/docs-theme), you'll need to run pnpm compile from packages/docs-data to see changes reflected in the application. For simplicity, an alias script pnpm docs-data exists in the root to minimize directory hopping.

## Architecture & Component Patterns

Blueprint JS organizes functionality into discrete packages (@blueprintjs/core, @blueprintjs/datetime, @blueprintjs/icons, @blueprintjs/table). This modular structure enables selective imports, reducing bundle size. Each package exposes React components with isolated responsibilities, promoting reusability.(no content)Styling leverages CSS variables scoped under .bp4-* classes for theme consistency. The framework ships with light/dark themes, customizable via overriding variables.(no content)Components follow React composition patterns, accepting children props and configuration via TypeScript interfaces. Components internally manage state transitions (e.g., loading spinners) while exposing controlled props for external manipulation.

## TypeScript Integration

Blueprint made an early bet on TypeScript; this choice proved to be instrumental for providing a superior developer experience compared to JavaScript. Rather than thinking about API contracts implicitly and trying to build documentation after the fact, TypeScript forces developers to be more explicit about assumptions and makes it easy to keep high quality, up-to-date docs.

## Development Requirements

Prerequisites: Node.js v20.11+ (see version specified in .nvmrc), pnpm v10.x (see version specified in package.json).

For more details, you can visit the official documentation at [blueprintjs.com/docs](https://blueprintjs.com/docs/) or the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/palantir/blueprint).

A Complete Guide - SixSigma.us","url":"https://www.6sigma.us/six-sigma-in-focus/agile-acceptance-criteria/"},{"title":"How To Write Excellent Acceptance Criteria (With Examples)","url":"https://cpoclub.com/product-development/how-to-write-excellent-acceptance-criteria-with-examples/"}]

Based on my search, here's what I found about wireframe acceptance criteria specification formats in 2025:

## Key Formats for Acceptance Criteria

### 1. Given-When-Then (Gherkin) Format
The given when then acceptance criteria format provides a simple pattern for writing acceptance tests for user stories: Given some context, When some action is carried out, Then the observable consequences should follow. Invented in the early 2000s and codified by the Agile Alliance, this template guides teams toward shared understanding before development begins.(no content)Over 60% of agile teams have adopted BDD practices to enhance collaboration and ensure software behaviors align with user needs, according to the 2024 World Quality Report.

### 2. Best Practices for Writing Acceptance Criteria

Stick to a single format (e.g., Given-When-Then or bullet points). Keep AC concise (3-5 criteria per feature is ideal). Align AC with user stories for clarity.(no content)Ensure your criteria are specific and measurable. Implement the SMART framework (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) for writing acceptance criteria to reduce ambiguity.

### 3. Integrating Wireframes with Acceptance Criteria

Acceptance Criteria (also called Conditions of Satisfaction, Acceptance Tests, etc.) is the third part of a User Story structure. This section is both for the developers and the testers.(no content)Wireframes can be created for an end-to-end workflow, leaving gaps for areas that are open to trigger discussion points. Walking through a new workflow using visuals helps elicit hidden business rules, alternate paths and creates good discussions.

### 4. AI-Assisted Approaches (2025)

Show & Hide the Right Information | UXPin","url":"https://www.uxpin.com/studio/blog/what-is-progressive-disclosure/"},{"title":"What is Progressive Disclosure? | IxDF","url":"https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/topics/progressive-disclosure"},{"title":"Progressive Disclosure | The Glossary of Human Computer Interaction","url":"https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/book/the-glossary-of-human-computer-interaction/progressive-disclosure"},{"title":"Progressive disclosure - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Progressive_disclosure"},{"title":"UX Database Newsletter #04 - Progressive vs Staged Disclosure","url":"https://www.uxdatabase.io/newsletter-issue/04-staged-vs-progressive-disclosure"},{"title":"The Power of Progressive Disclosure in SaaS UX Design","url":"https://lollypop.design/blog/2025/may/progressive-disclosure/"},{"title":"Progressive disclosure in UX design: Types and use cases - LogRocket Blog","url":"https://blog.logrocket.com/ux-design/progressive-disclosure-ux-types-use-cases/"},{"title":"Progressive Disclosure design pattern","url":"https://ui-patterns.com/patterns/ProgressiveDisclosure"},{"title":"Design Patterns: Progressive Disclosure for Mobile Apps | by Nick Babich | UX Planet","url":"https://uxplanet.org/design-patterns-progressive-disclosure-for-mobile-apps-f41001a293ba"}]

Here's what I found about the progressive disclosure UX pattern for enterprise applications:

## Definition

Progressive disclosure defers advanced or rarely used features to a secondary screen, making applications easier to learn and less error-prone.(no content)It's a technique UX designers use to reduce cognitive load by gradually revealing more complex information or features as the user progresses through a user interface.

## Key Benefits

Progressive disclosure helps maintain focus and attention of users by reducing clutter, confusion, and cognitive workload. It ramps up the experience, moving from simple to complex, from abstract to specific.(no content)Important content is prioritized—in an app designed with progressive disclosure, the very fact that something appears on the initial screen tells users that it's important. This leads to clean, simpler, more productive interfaces by hiding infrequently used features, removing UI clutter but not features.

## Application in Enterprise/Software

Progressive disclosure has long been one of application design's primary guidelines, because most applications have so many commands, features, and options that it makes sense to defer some to a secondary area.(no content)This strategy has been in use for more than 30 years. Its purpose is feature management, creating a flexible system adapted to junior users who do not know how to use the application, minimizing the error rate while giving experienced users access to a powerful toolbox.

## Common UI Patterns Used

Designers use UI patterns like modal windows and accordions to hide advanced features and information. This approach keeps the primary UI straightforward and inviting.(no content)UI patterns like dropdowns and accordions are great for progressive disclosure as they reveal details only when users want them. For example, Google Flights prioritizes key information such as flight times, duration, number of stops, and price, and users can click the dropdown to reveal additional details.(no content)Layering content presents information in a hierarchy, with the most important information presented first and less important information revealed as the user interacts. UI patterns like tabs and scrolling can help layer content effectively.

## Best Practices

Keep important information visible. Define essential and advanced content through user research using methods like card sorting and task analysis.(no content)Limit layers of information—a single secondary screen is typically sufficient for each instance of progressive disclosure.(no content)The main danger of progressive disclosure is incorrect assumption that you understand what is the most popular, common, or important task. When designing an app, it's easy to assume that everybody is like you.

## Classic Example

The print dialog box is the classic example of progressive disclosure. When you issue the command to print a document, you get a dialog box with a small set of choices—mainly how many copies to print, but possibly a few other variations.(no content)The initial print dialog box typically contains buttons for advanced options that lead to secondary dialogs for rarely used settings, such as scaling and printing pages in reverse sequence.

Here's what I found regarding dark mode accessibility guidelines and WCAG:

## Key WCAG Requirements for Dark Mode Accessibility

### Contrast Ratios
The WCAG guidelines emphasize several principles when designing for accessibility in dark mode: Contrast Ratios: Ensure text has a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 against the background for normal text and 3:1 for large text.(no content)The appropriate level of contrast for text to be accessible is 4.5:1 for normal text, and for large text (18 point and above or 14 point bold and above), the ratio is 3:1.

### Avoiding Pure Black and White
Pure black (hex #000000) is very high contrast and can cause eye strain, reduce legibility, and have a "halation effect." Choose softer shades of black or dark grays, and make sure the contrast ratio is 4.5:1 to meet WCAG 1.4.3.(no content)Avoid Pure Black: Use dark greys (e.g. #121212) instead of pure black (#000000) to reduce eye strain.

### Focus Indicators
This WCAG success criterion details techniques to help ensure focus indicators in black mode are visible. A11Y Check: 2.4.7 Focus Visible (Level AA) - Keyboard focus indicator must be visible.

### Dark Mode Is Not a WCAG Compliance Fix
Most importantly, high-contrast settings do not satisfy WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.3, "Contrast (Minimum)." This criterion doesn't include exceptions for websites that offer users an optional dark mode — it simply requires accessible contrast for all non-incidental text.

### Auditing Considerations
Providing the method of switching meets Text and Non-text contrast in all themes AND the alternate themes are "opt-in", WCAG criterions would be considered met if only assessed against the default theme. If themes are changed automatically without user interaction then all automatic themes must be assessed independently.

### European Accessibility Act (2025)
The date of 28th June 2025 is when the European Accessibility Act comes into force in each country in the EU. The EAA is an EU directive: a requirement for each individual country to implement the directive as a local law. There will finally be a legal requirement for accessibility in the private sector for a range of digital products and services, including banking, ebooks, travel tickets, and ecommerce sites.(no content)The requirement from clause 9 is for websites to reach WCAG 2.1 level-AA.

### Accessibility Challenges with Dark Mode
Some users, especially those with astigmatism or certain visual impairments, may find it harder to read white or brightly colored text against a dark background. This contrast can cause blurred vision or visual distortions, leading to discomfort.(no content)Accessibility experts recommend offering users the option to choose between different modes to better meet a variety of individual needs.

Here are the search results for responsive design specification documentation for mobile-first enterprise in 2025:

## Key Findings

### Mobile-First Design Importance in 2025

Responsive web design ensures optimal user experiences across all devices, with mobile traffic now representing 58% of global web usage and Google prioritizing mobile-first indexing for search rankings.(no content)Responsive web design is no longer a "nice-to-have"—it's the standard expectation for every digital experience. With more than 60% of global web traffic coming from mobile devices and enterprises racing to deliver seamless, device-agnostic interfaces, building adaptive digital platforms is now mission-critical.

### Core Technical Specifications

**Breakpoint Strategies:**
A common strategy includes a mobile base (320-767px), tablet range (768-1023px), desktop (1024-1439px), and large desktop (1440px+). However, these ranges should be adjusted based on your specific content and design requirements rather than treated as universal standards.

**Touch Target Requirements:**
Design for fingers, not cursors. Touch interactions should feel natural and responsive, with a minimum 44px touch targets (Apple/Google standard).

### Enterprise Frameworks for 2025

Foundation by Zurb is described as "a powerful mobile-first framework with flexible layouts & accessibility features," best for large-scale enterprise & complex projects. It features Flexbox & Grid-based responsive design, mobile-first typography, forms, and buttons, plus ARIA support for accessibility compliance.(no content)Bootstrap's grid system, pre-designed components, and extensive documentation make it beginner-friendly yet powerful enough for complex enterprise solutions. In 2025, Bootstrap 5+ introduces utility APIs for greater customization and improved responsiveness.

### Progressive Enhancement Approach

Progressive enhancement is the backbone of mobile-first design. Rather than stripping away features to fit mobile screens, this approach starts with essential functionality and builds up. The mobile version serves as the foundation, containing all critical content and features. As screen size increases, additional elements and interactions are introduced to take advantage of the extra space and capabilities of larger devices.

### Testing & Quality Assurance Tools

For quality assurance, recommended testing tools include: Google PageSpeed Insights for Core Web Vitals assessment and mobile optimization recommendations, Google Mobile-Friendly Test for validating mobile usability, Google Search Console to monitor mobile usability issues and Core Web Vitals performance over time, and physical device testing on actual iOS and Android devices across different screen sizes and network conditions.

### Content Strategy Best Practices

Great responsive design isn't just about moving boxes around—it's about showing the right information at the right time. A smart content strategy is one of the most overlooked responsive design best practices. This means organizing your information so it's easy to understand on any device. You don't hide content on mobile; you prioritize it. You show the most important things first and let users dig for more if they want to. This is called "progressive disclosure."

Here are the search results for component documentation standards in Storybook design systems in 2025:

## Key Findings

### What is Storybook?
"Storybook is a powerful frontend workshop environment tool that allows teams to design, build, and organize UI components (and even full screens!) without getting tripped up over business logic and plumbing."

### Documentation Best Practices

Storybook's Docs addon auto-generates usage, props, and code snippets so designers, engineers, and PMs all see the same source of truth without digging through repos.(no content)Proper documentation transforms a set of components into a complete design system. Each element must be described so that any developer or designer immediately understands its capabilities, limitations, and usage rules.

### 2025 Standards and Trends

The evolution has progressed through 2022 with growth of component and token libraries and rise of Storybook, 2023–2024 with widespread transition to design tokens and documentation automation, and by 2025 a design system becomes a full ecosystem.(no content)Tools such as Storybook or Ladle are used to create interactive examples and visually explore component behavior. Automatic generation of component properties, presentation of multiple states, and clear usage recommendations help maintain a consistent quality standard. As a result, documentation becomes a practical tool that reduces errors, accelerates onboarding, and ensures predictable interfaces.

### Key Documentation Components

Well-documented components can speed up development time by 40%, maintain brand coherence across teams, allow new team members to contribute within days, and prevent design debt and technical inconsistencies.

### Testing and Quality Standards

Storybook provides several types of tests: Visual tests (visual regression tests) that create screenshots of every commit to catch UI inconsistencies, Accessibility tests that run code against WCAG standards, Interaction tests for checking interactivity and states, Test coverage examining code against industry standards, and Snapshot tests identifying markup changes.

### Storybook Documentation Features

Storybook's DocsPage is a "zero-config default documentation" that automates basic doc creation. Product and engineering teams can expand this documentation to create usage and guideline information.(no content)You incrementally document by saving component variations as stories. With Storybook Docs, you get reasonable docs generated automatically. Later, you can add rich prose via Markdown, MDX, or inline comments.

### Considerations for Comprehensive Documentation

The primary purpose of Storybook is to build and render UI components. Utilizing it as a complete documentation solution for a design system may be considered a workaround or hack. While Storybook offers some documentation capabilities, it is not designed explicitly for comprehensive and customizable documentation.(no content)While Storybook excels at turning components into living playgrounds, the most mature teams pair it with a purpose-built documentation layer. ## Key Findings

### What is Storybook?

### Documentation Best Practices

### 2025 Standards and Trends

The evolution has progressed through 2022 with growth of component and token libraries and rise of Storybook, 2023–2024 with widespread transition to design tokens and documentation automation, and by 2025 a design system becomes a full ecosystem.(no content)Tools such as Storybook or Ladle are used to create interactive examples and visually explore component behavior. Automatic generation of component properties, presentation of multiple states, and clear usage recommendations help maintain a consistent quality standard. As a result, documentation becomes a practical tool that reduces errors, accelerates onboarding, and ensures predictable interfaces.

### Key Documentation Components

Well-documented components can speed up development time by 40%, maintain brand coherence across teams, allow new team members to contribute within days, and prevent design debt and technical inconsistencies.

### Testing and Quality Standards

Storybook provides several types of tests: Visual tests (visual regression tests) that create screenshots of every commit to catch UI inconsistencies, Accessibility tests that run code against WCAG standards, Interaction tests for checking interactivity and states, Test coverage examining code against industry standards, and Snapshot tests identifying markup changes.

### Storybook Documentation Features

Storybook's DocsPage is a "zero-config default documentation" that automates basic doc creation. Based on my search, here's an overview of the latest research and techniques for user persona template validation in 2025:

## Key Validation Techniques

### 1. Triangulation Methods
Triangulation, particularly methodological triangulation, is used to validate and strengthen persona findings. Four main types are available: Methodological triangulation uses multiple research methods to validate initial findings.(no content)Given the small number of participants in qualitative methods, it is important to validate findings with a greater number of users using quantitative methods.

### 2. Mixed-Method Approach
Using a mixed-method approach when developing user personas helps reduce bias, increase validity, and capture a more accurate and holistic understanding of your personas than just proto-personas, which are based on assumptions, or purely qualitative personas.

### 3. Prototype Testing
Validate your personas by first creating prototypes that address the needs and pain points identified in your personas. Then test these prototypes with real users who match your persona profiles.(no content)If real users face different challenges or behave differently than your personas predicted, revise your personas accordingly. Early validation helps prevent building the wrong solutions based on inaccurate assumptions.

### 4. Multi-Step Validation Process
Confirm your persona's accuracy by combining multiple validation steps: conduct real customer interviews to test assumptions, run A/B tests on messaging and review survey analytics.(no content)Tools like Typeform can collect structured feedback, while metrics such as engagement rate and task success reveal whether the persona reflects real user behavior.

### 5. Team Alignment Workshops
Hold alignment workshops: Bring your team together – designers, researchers, and product leads – to review personas alongside existing research. Does everything match up? Are the personas clear and reflective of what you've learned? These workshops aren't just about critique, they're about collaboration and sharpening your personas.

### 6. Behavioral Data Comparison
Compare against behavioral trends: Look at any existing user behavior data. If a persona prioritizes speed but the data shows users often pause to read instructions, it's a signal to dig deeper.

### 7. AI-Assisted Validation (2025 Trend)
It is important to validate that what the AI has produced is realistic. Obviously, no persona is a true representation as it is a snapshot in time of a hypothetical user. However, we do want it to be as accurate as possible.(no content)Share your drafts with colleagues who interact regularly with real users — people in support cells or research teams. Where possible, test with a handful of users. Then cut anything that you can't defend or correct any errors that are identified.

## Best Practices for Ongoing Validation

Adopt a quarterly review cycle, especially if your market or product changes frequently. Every 3–6 months, analyze analytics trends, gather fresh user feedback and review campaign performance to ensure personas stay relevant and aligned with evolving customer needs.(no content)Incorporate the insights and feedback obtained from the validation process into the persona development. Personas are not set in stone. They may evolve over time. As you gather more user research and insights, continue to iterate the personas to keep them relevant.

content.text:
Now I have comprehensive research. Let me compile this into a detailed report of best practices for UX/Product specification documents in 2025-2026.

---

# UX/Product Specification Document Best Practices (2025-2026)

## Relatorio Executivo

Este documento apresenta as melhores praticas atuais (2025-2026) para documentacao de especificacoes UX/Produto, conforme esperado por um Senior UX Designer ou Product Manager. A pesquisa foi baseada em fontes como Nielsen Norman Group, W3C, Figma Community, e lideres de UX empresarial.

---

## 1. User Persona Best Practices

### 1.1 JTBD (Jobs to Be Done) Methodology

O framework JTBD examina tres dimensoes cruciais ([User Interviews](https://www.userinterviews.com/ux-research-field-guide-chapter/jobs-to-be-done-jtbd-framework)):

| Dimensao | Descricao | Exemplo |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **Functional Jobs** | Tarefas praticas que clientes precisam realizar | "Organizar entregaveis da equipe" |
| **Emotional Jobs** | Sentimentos que clientes querem experimentar ou evitar | "Sentir confianca na entrega do projeto" |
| **Social Jobs** | Como clientes querem ser percebidos por outros | "Ser visto como um lider organizado" |

**Formato de documentacao JTBD:**
```
Quando [situacao/contexto],
eu quero [motivacao/job],
para que eu possa [resultado esperado/beneficio].
```

### 1.2 Integracao JTBD + Personas

Especialistas modernos recomendam combinar ambas abordagens ([Delve.ai](https://www.delve.ai/blog/personas-jobs-to-be-done)):

> "Personas respondem ao 'quem', enquanto JTBD aborda o 'que' do que clientes querem, junto com situacao, contexto e solucao."

**Modelo Unificado 2025:**
- Dados demograficos (persona tradicional)
- Clareza motivacional (JTBD)
- Comportamentos observados
- Pontos de dor e gatilhos

### 1.3 Tecnicas de Validacao de Personas

([Interaction Design Foundation](https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/article/user-persona-guide), [Lyssna](https://www.lyssna.com/blog/persona-research/))

1. **Triangulacao Metodologica**: Usar multiplos metodos de pesquisa para validar descobertas
2. **Abordagem Mista**: Combinar metodos qualitativos e quantitativos
3. **Teste com Prototipos**: Validar personas testando prototipos com usuarios reais
4. **Workshops de Alinhamento**: Reunir equipe (designers, pesquisadores, PMs) para revisar personas
5. **Comparacao com Dados Comportamentais**: Analisar dados de comportamento real vs. persona
6. **Ciclo de Revisao Trimestral**: Revisar a cada 3-6 meses

---

## 2. User Journey Mapping

### 2.1 Evolucao em 2025

Journey mapping evoluiu de diagramas unicos para ferramentas de orquestracao em tempo real ([CMSWire](https://www.cmswire.com/customer-experience/customer-journey-mapping-a-how-to-guide/)):

> "O que torna um journey map eficaz agora e a especificidade comportamental. Em vez de estagios amplos como 'consciencia' e 'consideracao', estamos mapeando comportamentos reais do cliente."

### 2.2 Elementos Essenciais

| Elemento | Descricao |
|----------|-----------|
| **Personas** | Grupos de clientes baseados em demograficos e psicograficos |
| **Acoes** | O que o usuario faz em cada touchpoint |
| **Timeline** | Processo de passar pelos touchpoints e fases |
| **Emocoes** | Estado emocional em cada etapa |
| **Pain Points** | Pontos de friccao e dificuldade |
| **Oportunidades** | Areas de melhoria identificadas |

### 2.3 Service Blueprint vs Journey Map

([Miro](https://miro.com/customer-journey-map/service-blueprint-vs-journey-map/), [NN/g](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/service-blueprinting-faq/))

| Aspecto | Journey Map | Service Blueprint |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Perspectiva** | Cliente | Organizacao |
| **Objetivo** | Entender experiencia do usuario | Mapear processos internos |
| **Conteudo** | Pensamentos, emocoes, acoes | Frontstage, backstage, suporte |
| **Uso** | Entender "por que" | Entender "como" |

**Melhor pratica**: Use ambos juntos para alinhar insight do cliente com execucao operacional.

### 2.4 Sincronizacao Best Practices

1. **Workshops Cross-funcionais**: Reunir stakeholders de design de servico e UX
2. **Objetivos Compartilhados**: Estabelecer metas comuns para ambos os mapas
3. **Alinhamento Iterativo**: Revisar e atualizar ambos os mapas conforme servicos evoluem

---

## 3. Information Architecture

### 3.1 IA na Era da IA (2025)

([Medium - Samdiago](https://medium.com/@samdiago4516/what-is-information-architecture-building-smarter-enterprises-in-the-age-of-ai-e4dd933c54b1))

> "Ao gerenciar informacao estrategicamente, empresas criam a fundacao para IA, analytics e transformacao digital. Em seu nucleo, Information Architecture e sobre projetar a estrutura de dados para que possam ser acessados, entendidos e aplicados."

### 3.2 Frameworks Principais

| Framework | Descricao | Melhor para |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| **TOGAF** | Processo repetivel passo-a-passo com ADM | Implementacao estruturada |
| **Zachman** | Classificar, estruturar e analisar arquitetura | Documentacao clara de componentes |

### 3.3 Progressive Disclosure

([NN/g](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/progressive-disclosure/), [UXPin](https://www.uxpin.com/studio/blog/what-is-progressive-disclosure/))

**Definicao**: Tecnica que adia recursos avancados ou raramente usados para uma tela secundaria, tornando aplicacoes mais faceis de aprender e menos propensas a erros.

**Beneficios para Enterprise:**
- Mantem foco e atencao reduzindo desordem
- Rampa a experiencia de simples para complexo
- Prioriza conteudo importante
- Interfaces mais limpas e produtivas

**Padroes UI Comuns:**
- Modais e accordions para esconder recursos avancados
- Dropdowns para revelar detalhes sob demanda
- Tabs e scrolling para layering de conteudo
- Print dialog como exemplo classico

**Armadilha Principal**: Assumir incorretamente que voce entende qual e a tarefa mais popular ou comum.

---

## 4. Design System Documentation

### 4.1 Design Tokens - Especificacao Estavel 2025

([W3C Design Tokens Community Group](https://www.w3.org/community/design-tokens/2025/10/28/design-tokens-specification-reaches-first-stable-version/), [designtokens.org](https://www.designtokens.org/tr/drafts/format/))

Em **outubro de 2025**, a especificacao Design Tokens atingiu sua primeira versao estavel (2025.10), introduzindo:

- **Theming e suporte multi-marca**
- **Especificacao de cor moderna** com suporte completo para Display P3, Oklch, e CSS Color Module 4
- **Relacionamentos ricos entre tokens**
- **Consistencia cross-platform**

**Formato do Arquivo:**
```json
{
  "$type": "color",
  "$value": "#1d4ed8",
  "$description": "Primary brand color"
}
```

- Media type: `application/design-tokens+json`
- Extensoes: `.tokens` ou `.tokens.json`
- Propriedades prefixadas com `$`

### 4.2 Tipos de Tokens

([VA.gov Design System](https://design.va.gov/foundation/design-tokens))

| Tipo | Descricao | Exemplo |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **Primitives** | Building blocks basicos | `color.blue.500` |
| **Semantic** | Comunicam como primitivo e usado | `color.text.primary` |
| **Component** | Escopo especifico para componentes | `button.background.hover` |

### 4.3 Component Documentation Standards

([Storybook](https://storybook.js.org/), [Supernova](https://www.supernova.io/blog/top-storybook-documentation-examples-and-the-lessons-you-can-learn))

**Storybook DocsPage automatiza:**
- Geracao de uso e props
- Code snippets
- Estados e variantes
- Documentacao acessivel a designers, engenheiros e PMs

**Componentes bem documentados:**
- Aceleram desenvolvimento em 40%
- Mantem coerencia de marca entre equipes
- Permitem novos membros contribuir em dias
- Previnem design debt e inconsistencias tecnicas

**Tipos de Teste Integrados:**
- Visual tests (regressao visual)
- Accessibility tests (WCAG)
- Interaction tests
- Snapshot tests

### 4.4 Dark Mode Specification

([Medium - Ebunoluwa Ige](https://medium.com/@design.ebuniged/designing-accessible-dark-mode-a-wcag-compliant-interface-redesign-0e0225833aa4), [DubBot](https://dubbot.com/dubblog/2023/dark-mode-a11y.html), [BOIA](https://www.boia.org/blog/offering-a-dark-mode-doesnt-satisfy-wcag-color-contrast-requirements))

**Requisitos WCAG para Dark Mode:**

| Requisito | Especificacao |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Contraste texto normal** | Minimo 4.5:1 |
| **Contraste texto grande** | Minimo 3:1 |
| **Evitar preto puro** | Usar cinzas escuros (e.g., #121212) em vez de #000000 |
| **Focus Visible (2.4.7)** | Indicador de foco deve ser visivel |

**Importante**: Dark mode NAO satisfaz WCAG 1.4.3 automaticamente - contraste acessivel e requerido em TODOS os temas.

**European Accessibility Act (28 Junho 2025)**: Websites de e-commerce na UE devem atingir WCAG 2.1 nivel-AA.

### 4.5 Blueprint.js Patterns

([Blueprint.js Docs](https://blueprintjs.com/docs/), [GitHub](https://github.com/palantir/blueprint))

**Arquitetura:**
- Pacotes discretos (`@blueprintjs/core`, `@blueprintjs/datetime`, etc.)
- Imports seletivos para reduzir bundle size
- CSS variables sob classes `.bp4-*`
- Temas light/dark customizaveis
- TypeScript-first para documentacao superior

**Integracao com Documentacao:**
- JSDoc comments em arquivos `.tsx`
- KSS markup em arquivos `.scss`
- Extracao automatica via `documentalist`

---

## 5. Screen Specification Standards

### 5.1 Wireframe Documentation

([Balsamiq](https://balsamiq.com/learn/resources/articles/userstories/), [Bridging the Gap](https://www.bridging-the-gap.com/using-wireframes-or-prototypes-to-elicit-analyze-and-validate-software-requirements/))

**Integracao com User Stories:**
> "Wireframes podem ser criados para um workflow end-to-end, deixando gaps para areas abertas que disparam pontos de discussao."

**Propositos:**
- Elicitar regras de negocio ocultas
- Identificar caminhos alternativos
- Criar discussoes produtivas
- Servir como ponto de referencia para especificacoes funcionais

### 5.2 Acceptance Criteria Formats

([Parallel HQ](https://www.parallelhq.com/blog/given-when-then-acceptance-criteria), [Olutobi](https://olutobi.com/2025/03/26/writing-acceptance-criteria-in-product-development/))

**Formato Given-When-Then (Gherkin):**
```gherkin
Given [contexto inicial]
When [acao executada]
Then [resultado observavel]
```

> "Mais de 60% das equipes ageis adotaram praticas BDD para melhorar colaboracao." - World Quality Report 2024

**Best Practices:**
- Manter consistencia em um formato
- Manter AC conciso (3-5 criterios por feature)
- Alinhar AC com user stories
- Implementar framework SMART
- Incluir cenarios negativos
- Quantificar performance (ex: "busca retorna em menos de 200ms")

### 5.3 Responsive Design Specifications

([UI Deploy](https://ui-deploy.com/blog/complete-responsive-web-design-guide-mobile-first-development-and-breakpoint-strategies-2025), [Next Native](https://nextnative.dev/blog/responsive-design-best-practices))

**Estrategia de Breakpoints:**

| Range | Dispositivo | Pixels |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| Base | Mobile | 320-767px |
| Tablet | Tablet | 768-1023px |
| Desktop | Desktop | 1024-1439px |
| Large | Large Desktop | 1440px+ |

**Requisitos Touch:**
- Minimum touch targets: **44px** (padrao Apple/Google)

**Progressive Enhancement:**
- Versao mobile como fundacao
- Elementos adicionais conforme tela aumenta
- Progressive disclosure para conteudo

### 5.4 Componentes de uma Spec Completa

([Interaction Design Foundation](https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/topics/design-specifications), [UX Prep](https://www.uiprep.com/blog/the-best-way-to-document-ux-ui-design))

| Secao | Conteudo |
|-------|----------|
| **Project Overview** | Escopo, objetivos, roadmap |
| **Typography** | Estilos de caracteres, fonts |
| **Colors** | Background, foreground, semanticos |
| **Measurements** | Espacamentos, grid, breakpoints |
| **Component States** | Todas as variantes e estados |
| **User Flows** | Fluxos de navegacao |
| **Accessibility** | Tab order, alt text, ARIA |
| **Error States** | Mensagens e comportamentos de erro |

---

## 6. Tendencias 2025-2026

### 6.1 AI Integration

([Medium - Xperienz](https://medium.com/@xperienzRD/5-trends-shaping-ux-in-2026-61a77f9e641f))

> "Na UXLx 2025, John Whalen demonstrou como um processo de pesquisa que tradicionalmente levava 35 dias em sete semanas podia ser completado em menos de uma semana com suporte de IA."

### 6.2 Web Sustainability Guidelines

([W3C](https://www.w3.org/community/design-tokens/))

- Finalizacao esperada em **abril de 2026**
- Cobertura: UX, desenvolvimento, e decisoes estrategicas
- Objetivo: experiencias limpas, eficientes, acessiveis, resilientes

### 6.3 Multimodal Interaction

> "Bom design UX em 2026 considera contexto primeiro. Pessoas usam produtos enquanto andam, cozinham, dirigem, ou trocam de dispositivos."

---

## 7. Template de Documento Spec (Estrutura Recomendada)

```markdown
# [Nome do Produto/Feature] - UX Specification

## 1. Resumo Executivo
- Visao geral
- Objetivos de negocio
- Metricas de sucesso

## 2. Personas & JTBD
- Persona primaria (com validacao)
- Jobs-to-be-done funcionais, emocionais, sociais
- Cenarios de uso

## 3. Journey Maps
- Journey map atual (as-is)
- Journey map futuro (to-be)
- Service blueprint (se aplicavel)

## 4. Information Architecture
- Sitemap/Navigation structure
- Progressive disclosure strategy
- Content hierarchy

## 5. Design Tokens
- Primitives (cores, tipografia, espacamento)
- Semantic tokens
- Component tokens

## 6. Wireframes & Screen Specs
- Low-fidelity wireframes
- High-fidelity mockups
- Estados de componentes
- Responsive behavior

## 7. Acceptance Criteria
- Given-When-Then format
- Edge cases
- Error handling

## 8. Accessibility
- WCAG compliance checklist
- Keyboard navigation
- Screen reader behavior
- Dark mode specs

## 9. Handoff Notes
- Developer annotations
- Animation specs
- Performance requirements
```

---

## Sources

### UX Documentation & Specifications
- [Interaction Design Foundation - Design Specifications](https://www.interaction-design.org/literature/topics/design-specifications)
- [Nielsen Norman Group - Creating Design Specs for Development](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/creating-design-specs-for-development/)
- [UX Prep - The Best Way to Document UX/UI Design](https://www.uiprep.com/blog/the-best-way-to-document-ux-ui-design)
- [LogRocket - UX Documentation Guide](https://blog.logrocket.com/ux-design/ux-documentation-guide-best-practices-template/)

### JTBD & Personas
- [Nielsen Norman Group - Personas vs. Jobs-to-Be-Done](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/personas-jobs-be-done/)
